target.title: feat(cli-output): tool-use injection + rolling-timer indicator for claude-stream-json backends
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem.** When Claude calls a tool mid-turn on the `claude-cli` backend, the assistant text stream just pauses — often for tens of seconds on `Read` / `Bash` / `Grep` / `WebFetch`. The end user sees a frozen typing indicator and no signal about which tool is running.
- **Why it matters.** Looks like the gateway hung; users abandon turns that are still in flight. Tool calls live in `stream_event` records, never in `assistant_text` deltas, so the existing assistant-stream consumer never surfaces them.
- **What changed.** Two complementary surfaces on `createCliJsonlStreamingParser` (gated by `usesClaudeStreamJsonDialect`):
  1. **In-stream textual marker** via `onAssistantDelta` — `\n\n[HH:MM:SS] 🛠️ ToolName: detail\n` followed by a rolling `_ Ns — HH:MM:SS_` indicator that refreshes every 8s, stripped on the next `text_delta` or on turn end.
  2. **Structured `stream: "tool"` agent event** (`{phase: "start", name, args, itemId}`) via a new optional `onToolEvent` callback — consumed by `cli-runner/execute.ts` and forwarded to the agent event bus for downstream tool-tile UIs.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary).** Gated by `usesClaudeStreamJsonDialect({backend, providerId})` — no impact on non-Claude CLI backends. Existing `onAssistantDelta` callers are unaffected. `onToolEvent` is opt-in. No tool-output / tool-result event in this PR (the result is reflected in claude's next `text_delta` and the timer-strip handles UX). Tool args sanitized via `sanitizeToolArgs` before either path.
- **AI-assisted:** yes — drafted with Claude.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** tool calls during a Claude turn now produce both an in-stream textual marker (with rolling 8s timer indicator) and a structured `stream: "tool"` agent event, when `backend.jsonlDialect === "claude-stream-json"` or `providerId === "claude-cli"`.
- **Real environment tested:** Windows 11 Pro 26200, Node v24.12.0, pnpm, `@anthropic-ai/claude-code` v2.1.142, Opus 4.7, live OpenClaw gateway driving the `claude-cli` backend per-turn over Telegram.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p tsconfig.json`; live Telegram turn that invokes one or more tools (Read / Bash / Grep / WebFetch); observe the textual marker, the 8s tick refresh, and the marker stripping plus `\n\n` separator when assistant text resumes; verify the `stream: "tool"` agent event in `emitAgentEvent` logs.
- **Evidence after fix:**
  - Telegram screenshot of a live turn (rolling-timer rendered to the end-user surface): ![telegram rolling-timer](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets/pr-82285/.proof/telegram-rolling-timer.jpg). Every `[HH:MM:SS] 🛠️ Bash: …` line in the screenshot is one `emitTool` firing in `createClaudeToolUseTracker` against `claude-cli`.
  - Typecheck clean: `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p tsconfig.json`.
  - The markers are an in-flight overlay delivered via `onAssistantDelta`, not persisted to `~/.openclaw/agents/main/sessions/*.jsonl` (which stores claude's structured `result`) or `~/.openclaw/logs/gateway.log` at default verbosity — Telegram is the canonical capture surface.
- **Observed result after fix:** tool calls render as `[HH:MM:SS] 🛠️ ToolName: detail` blocks in the assistant text, with the live timer ticking every 8s. When claude resumes streaming text, the timer is stripped and the text continues from a clean `\n\n` boundary. Agent-event consumers receive a structured `{phase: "start", name, args, itemId}` for each tool call.
- **What was not tested:** end-to-end against a dashboard / external listener for the `stream: "tool"` event surface (event is emitted, visible in `emitAgentEvent` logs, but no downstream UI verified beyond that); Linux/macOS execution (developed on Windows only); bundled MCP tool paths (`server_tool_use` / `mcp_tool_use` content-block types are matched in the tracker but I have not driven a live MCP-tool turn through it); focused unit test for `createClaudeToolUseTracker` is not added yet (see Regression Test Plan).

## Change Type

- [x] Feature

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Integrations

## Linked

- Adds opt-in `onToolEvent` callback on `createCliJsonlStreamingParser` + in-stream textual marker over the existing `onAssistantDelta`.
- Overlap to coordinate: #80046 (CLI tool-event bridge). Maintainers' judgment on whether inline rolling markers ride alongside or after the structured bridge.

## Root Cause

N/A — feature PR. The condition this addresses (no tool-call UX during turns) is a property of the Claude CLI JSONL surface (tool calls live in `stream_event`, never in `assistant_text` deltas), not a regression in OpenClaw.

## Regression Test Plan

- **Target:** `src/agents/cli-output.test.ts` — add a `describe("tool-use tracker", …)` block.
- **Scenario locked in:** simulated `stream_event` sequence — `message_start` → `content_block_start (tool_use, name: "Read")` → 3× `content_block_delta (input_json_delta partial)` → `content_block_stop` — asserts the resulting `ClaudeToolEvent` carries the reassembled `args.file_path`, the right `itemId`, and that the parser's `assistantText` now includes the `[HH:MM:SS] 🛠️ Read: <path>` line.
- **Why smallest reliable guardrail:** the failure mode (regression of partial-JSON accumulation, wrong `content_block_stop` dispatch, verbose-gating drift) is fully exercisable by calling the parser's `push()` with a canned JSONL transcript — no live claude needed. Runs in ms.
- **Honest gap:** I have **not** added this test yet. Behaviour is exercised live in my local environment but a frozen-transcript unit test belongs with this PR before flipping out of draft. Flagging as a must-add before un-drafting.

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** partial-JSON accumulation in `createClaudeToolUseTracker` could drop or corrupt tool args if `content_block_stop` fires before all `input_json_delta` chunks arrive.
  - **Mitigation:** tracker holds the entry until `content_block_stop`, then parses `entry.input ?? entry.partialJson` via `parseClaudeToolArgs`. The pending unit test (`cli-output.test.ts`) locks in the contract for partial-JSON reassembly.
- **Risk:** rolling-timer `setInterval` leaks if a `content_block_stop` arrives but no subsequent `text_delta` fires (e.g. turn aborts mid-tool).
  - **Mitigation:** `clearToolKeepalive()` is also called on `result`, on `finish()`, and on the next `text_delta`. The parser closure owns the interval handle and explicitly clears it on every terminal path.
- **Risk:** tool args contain sensitive content (file paths, credentials in `WebFetch` URLs, etc.) and surface in both Telegram and agent events.
  - **Mitigation:** `sanitizeToolArgs` truncates and redacts known-sensitive keys before either emission path. Verbose gating (`shouldInjectToolInlineMarkers`) lets sessions opt out of inline markers without losing the structured event.
- **Risk:** the inline marker rides on `onAssistantDelta` — any consumer that strict-parses assistant text as Markdown could choke on the `_ Ns — HH:MM:SS_` indicator.
  - **Mitigation:** marker uses a deliberately conservative `\n\n[…] 🛠️ …\n` shape that's Markdown-safe (Telegram renders it verbatim). Consumers wanting strict text can ignore by subscribing only to the structured `stream: "tool"` event.

## Security Impact

- **New permissions/capabilities:** No.
- **Secrets/tokens handling changed:** No — same allowed-tools set; `sanitizeToolArgs` truncates args before emission.
- **New/changed network calls:** No.
- **Command/tool execution surface changed:** No — same tools claude already executes; this PR only renders that they're in flight.
- **Data access scope changed:** No — tracker reads `stream_event` records the parser already processes.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible: gated by `usesClaudeStreamJsonDialect`; non-Claude backends unaffected.
- `onToolEvent` is an additive optional callback — existing parser callers don't need to wire it up.
- No new config, no new env vars. Disable by not subscribing to the new event surface.

target.title: feat(anthropic): claude-cli-interactive backend — stream reasoning via local TLS proxy (experimental)
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem.** OpenClaw's `claude-cli` backend invokes `claude -p --output-format stream-json` (headless). Starting **June 15, 2026** that path moves onto Anthropic's separate programmatic credit pool ([VentureBeat coverage](https://venturebeat.com/technology/anthropic-reinstates-openclaw-and-third-party-agent-usage-on-claude-subscriptions-with-a-catch)) — fixed monthly allowance, billed at API rates, doesn't roll over.
- **Why it matters.** Every OpenClaw Opus turn on a Claude subscription will drain that fixed credit post–June 15 instead of counting against the interactive subscription limits subscribers are paying for. Opus turns exhaust the credit fast.
- **What changed.** New opt-in `claude-cli-interactive` backend. Each turn spawns one fresh `bun wrapper.ts …` that boots a loopback HTTPS MITM on a 127.0.0.1 port, taps every API SSE event, re-emits the events as `claude -p --output-format stream-json` JSONL records, and kills the claude child on `end_turn`. A `tty-spoof.cjs` `NODE_OPTIONS=--require` preload keeps claude in interactive mode under piped stdio.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary).** Existing `claude-cli` backend untouched. `agents.defaults.cliBackends.claude-cli` user overrides inherit into the interactive backend via `inheritUserConfigFrom` with `-p`-mode flags stripped. System CA store is not mutated (`NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS` scoped to the spawned claude child only). Non–`api.anthropic.com` CONNECT tunnels pass through unmodified. Tool-use rendering / waiting-on-tool injection lands in #82285, not here.
- **AI-assisted:** yes — drafted with Claude.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** `claude` running under OpenClaw in interactive (subscription) mode with full `init` / `stream_event*` / `result` JSONL reconstruction on the wrapper's stdout, sourced from the loopback MITM proxy's SSE tap.
- **Real environment tested:** Windows 11 Pro 26200, Node v24.12.0, pnpm, Bun, `@anthropic-ai/claude-code` v2.1.142 (npm-installed), Opus 4.7 (`claude-opus-4-7`), live OpenClaw gateway driving the backend per-turn over Telegram.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `unset NODE_OPTIONS && pnpm exec vitest run extensions/anthropic/cli-backend-interactive.test.ts`; `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/anthropic/tsconfig.json`; set `cliBackends.claude-cli-interactive` active in user config; restart the OpenClaw gateway; send a Telegram message that triggers an Opus 4.7 turn; observe wrapper boot in stderr, SSE flow through `127.0.0.1:<port>`, JSONL arriving at OpenClaw; send a follow-up message with `--resume <sessionId>` to verify resume.
- **Evidence after fix:**
  - vitest: `Test Files 1 passed (1) / Tests 5 passed (5)` — `normalizeConfig` branches (inherited claude-cli match, direct Bun-path override, env passthrough, no-override skip).
  - Gateway log excerpt + cert listing + active-config snapshot on the [`proof-assets/pr-81851`](https://github.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/tree/proof-assets/pr-81851/.proof) branch. Embedded sample (one of three real turns in the gateway-log excerpt):

    ```
    cli exec: provider=claude-cli-interactive model=opus useResume=true session=present
    cli argv: bun <NPM>\…\wrapper.ts --effort max --verbose --setting-sources user --resume <SESSION> --allowedTools mcp__openclaw__* --model opus <prompt:N chars>
    [openclaw-proxy] certs ready: <HOME>\.openclaw\proxy-certs\ca.crt
    [openclaw-proxy] MITM proxy active on 127.0.0.1:17081 — Anthropic API traffic intercepted locally to capture thinking_delta events
    [openclaw-proxy] spawning claude binary: <NPM>\…\@anthropic-ai\claude-code\bin\claude.exe
    [openclaw-proxy] result flushed, waiting for session write before kill
    [openclaw-proxy] session file updated, killing claude
    ```
- **Observed result after fix:** per-turn lifecycle terminates cleanly — wrapper emits `init`, a continuous stream of `stream_event` records, then a single `result` on `end_turn`, sets `intentionalKill = true`, and `claude.kill()`s the child so the per-turn process tree exits 0.
- **What was not tested:** Linux/macOS (developed on Windows 11 only); bundled-MCP `claude-config-file` mode end-to-end with multiple MCP servers active (only `mcp__openclaw__*` exercised live); forced Anthropic-side `429` / `overloaded_error` mid-stream (the `api_error` synthetic event path is implemented but I did not provoke a real 429).

## Change Type

- [x] Feature

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Integrations

## Linked

- Related: #81550 (programmatic-mode reasoning bridge — this PR is the interactive-mode equivalent on a separate backend); #82285 (tool-use injection, builds on the `stream_event` surface this PR establishes).

## Root Cause

N/A — feature PR. The lack of thinking visibility on subscription is a property of `claude -p` (headless) mode, not a regression in OpenClaw.

## Regression Test Plan

- **Target:** `extensions/anthropic/cli-backend-interactive.test.ts`.
- **Scenario locked in:** `normalizeConfig` post-merge shape across four branches — inherited claude-cli match (forces `command="bun"`, stashes binary on env), direct Bun-path override (keeps the override as the spawn command, no env stash), env passthrough preservation, no-override case (env passthrough omitted so wrapper falls back to PATH lookup of `"claude"`).
- **Why smallest reliable guardrail:** the failure mode is config-shape drift inside `normalizeConfig` — fully exercised synchronously without spawning bun or claude, runs in ms vs the minutes an e2e run would cost.

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** newly-installed user with no inherited `claude-cli` override could spawn `bun` as the claude binary if `normalizeConfig` stashed `merged.command` indiscriminately.
  - **Mitigation:** `normalizeConfig` consults `context.config.agents.defaults.cliBackends.claude-cli.command`; only stashes the env passthrough on a match. Regression test asserts the env var is `undefined` for the no-override branch.
- **Risk:** `tty-spoof.cjs` is a runtime support file (not a build output) and could be omitted from packaged installs.
  - **Mitigation:** declared in `extensions/anthropic/package.json#openclaw.build.staticAssets`; postbuild asset copier ships it alongside the wrapper.
- **Risk:** winget-installed `claude.exe` shadows the npm install via PATHEXT on Windows.
  - **Mitigation:** wrapper spawns `OPENCLAW_INTERACTIVE_CLAUDE_BINARY` directly when the parent backend resolved a binary, never falling back to a bare PATH lookup.
- **Risk:** title-gen / Haiku side-requests inject `{"title":"…"}` artifacts into the reasoning stream.
  - **Mitigation:** mitm-server classifies each `/v1/messages` request into `auxiliary` (no tools) / `compaction` (compact.ts prompt markers) / `tool_followup` (last msg has tool_result) / `normal`. Wrapper drops auxiliary entirely, streams compaction as `thinking_delta`, emits a result only on `normal` `end_turn`. Response-content fingerprint backup (`<analysis>` + Primary-Request-and-Intent + Pending-Tasks) catches anything that slipped past the request-level classifier.
- **Risk:** spilled-prompt overflow file leaks to concurrent runs via shared tempdir + `--add-dir`.
  - **Mitigation:** per-run subdir under `${tmpdir()}/openclaw-interactive-proxy/run-<32-hex-bytes>/` (0700), file 0600, `--add-dir` scoped to the per-run subdir only.
- **Risk:** local CA + leaf key persisted under user's OpenClaw config dir could be reused if an attacker gets read access.
  - **Mitigation:** trust scoped to the spawned claude child via `NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS` only (no system trust mutation); CA issues solely the one `api.anthropic.com` leaf the per-turn proxy needs; key files hardened to 0600 with fail-closed regeneration; maintainer security review invited.
- **Risk:** Anthropic-side non-SSE 4xx/5xx on `/v1/messages` (429, billing, overloaded) hangs the wrapper because claude doesn't self-exit on API error.
  - **Mitigation:** mitm-server emits a synthetic `api_error` event for non-SSE 4xx/5xx; wrapper surfaces a `result` with `is_error: true` and exits nonzero so the CLI runner treats the turn as failed and triggers failover.
- **Risk:** two resumes of the same Claude session race on the same transcript.
  - **Mitigation:** backend config sets `serialize: true` so the framework queues same-session runs; different sessions still run concurrently. mitm-server binds both proxy stages on port 0 so concurrent proxies can't collide on a preferred port either.

## Security Impact

- **New permissions/capabilities:** Yes — per-turn local TLS MITM on a loopback port. Scoped to the spawned claude child via `NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS`; system trust store untouched. Local CA + leaf key cached under user's OpenClaw config dir at 0700/0600.
- **Secrets/tokens handling changed:** No — wrapper does not read, log, or modify the Anthropic auth token; it is forwarded byte-for-byte to the re-issued upstream request.
- **New/changed network calls:** Yes — claude's outbound HTTPS to `api.anthropic.com:443` is rerouted via `HTTPS_PROXY=http://127.0.0.1:<port>` inside the child. Non–`api.anthropic.com` CONNECT tunnels (statsig, datadog, etc.) bypass the TLS terminator entirely.
- **Command/tool execution surface changed:** No — same `claude` binary, same allowed-tools (`mcp__openclaw__*`), same session semantics.
- **Data access scope changed:** No — proxy sees only the SSE bytes claude itself sees on the wire; nothing exfiltrated or aggregated outside the per-turn process.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible: existing `claude-cli` backend unchanged. Opt-in via the `claude-cli-interactive` id; no default flips.
- New optional env: `OPENCLAW_INTERACTIVE_PROXY_DEBUG=1` enables verbose wrapper stderr.
- Migration needed: no.

## Prior art

[`smithersai/claude-p`](https://github.com/smithersai/claude-p) runs `claude` without `-p` but bypasses the JSONL streaming surface entirely — works for the raw final text but loses the `stream_event` records that reasoning and tool-use rendering depend on. This PR reproduces the `-p --output-format stream-json` shape record-for-record from the SSE tap so existing OpenClaw stream consumers layer on unchanged.

target.title: fix(cron): capture originating session/agent on the cron wake tool call
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem.** The cron `wake` MCP tool forwards only `{mode, text}` to the gateway, so every wake enqueues against the heartbeat/main default. Wakes scheduled from a non-main session (Telegram thread, Discord channel, multi-agent setup) silently route to the wrong conversation lane — on CLI runtimes the fired wake spawns a fresh session and burns tokens generating output nothing can route back; on embedded the wake fires the agent's default session, not the originating one.
- **What changed.** Wake tool resolves `opts.agentSessionKey` via the existing `resolveInternalSessionKey` / `resolveSessionAgentId` pattern (mirrors the `add` action at L569-583) and threads `sessionKey` + `agentId` through `WakeParamsSchema` → gateway handler → `context.cron.wake` → `enqueueSystemEvent` + `requestHeartbeat`. Explicit params on the tool call take precedence over inferred values.
- **CLI runtime coverage (claude-cli + claude-cli-interactive).** No CLI-specific changes — the runner's `useResume` is already auto-derived from `cliSessionId` + `backend.resumeArgs` presence (`src/agents/cli-runner/execute.ts:276-277`). Once the wake's `sessionKey` reaches the cron service, the heartbeat-runner targets the right session lane → the cron isolated agent resolves the right `sessionEntry` → `getCliSessionId` returns that session's claude-side id → `--resume <sessionId>` flips on automatically for any backend that declares `resumeArgs` (both Claude backends do). Same path works for Codex, Gemini-CLI, etc.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary).** `WakeParamsSchema` still permits `additionalProperties: true`. Pre-existing call sites that omit origin still route to the dep's default (backwards compatible). Subagent session keys reject early with `unwakeable-session-key` (upstream guard, preserved). Channel/thread/topic capture on the job's `delivery` block stays a follow-up — this PR's session-key bridge unblocks the wake-side routing without touching the delivery contract.
- **AI-assisted:** yes — drafted with Claude.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** cron `wake` invoked by an agent inside a non-main session threads the originating session/agent through the wake gateway call so the system event lands on that session's queue and the targeted heartbeat picks it up. CLI runtimes (both `claude-cli` headless and `claude-cli-interactive`) consume the resolved session through the existing `useResume` auto-derivation, so the woken claude process resumes the originating conversation rather than spawning fresh.
- **Real environment tested:** Windows 11 Pro 26200, Node v24.12.0, pnpm, AJV-compiled TypeBox validators, vitest 4.1.6. Live harness in `scripts/proof-cron-wake-origin.mts` drives the patched gateway wake handler (`validateWakeParams` + `isSubagentSessionKey` guard) through the patched `cron.wake()` / `timer.ts` with logging deps that print every `enqueueSystemEvent` / `requestHeartbeat` call.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `unset NODE_OPTIONS && pnpm exec vitest run src/cron/service/wake-origin.test.ts src/gateway/protocol/index.test.ts && pnpm exec tsx scripts/proof-cron-wake-origin.mts`.
- **Evidence after fix:** unit-test suite — `Test Files 3 passed (3) / Tests 82 passed (82) / Duration 8.05s`. Live harness stdout (synthetic ids; real chat identifiers redacted at source — the script never references real ones):

  ```
  === non-main session + non-default agent (the bug-fix case) ===
  > wake params: {"mode":"now","text":"follow up on report","sessionKey":"agent:coding:telegram:<chat-id-redacted>:topic:<topic-id-redacted>","agentId":"coding"}
  [gateway/cron] enqueueSystemEvent  text="follow up on report"  opts={"sessionKey":"agent:coding:telegram:<chat-id-redacted>:topic:<topic-id-redacted>","agentId":"coding"}
  [gateway/heartbeat] requestHeartbeat  {"source":"manual","intent":"immediate","reason":"wake","sessionKey":"agent:coding:telegram:<chat-id-redacted>:topic:<topic-id-redacted>","agentId":"coding"}
  < wake result:  ok=true  payload={"ok":true}

  === no origin (backwards-compatible default routing) ===
  > wake params: {"mode":"now","text":"ping"}
  [gateway/cron] enqueueSystemEvent  text="ping"  opts=undefined
  [gateway/heartbeat] requestHeartbeat  {"source":"manual","intent":"immediate","reason":"wake"}
  < wake result:  ok=true  payload={"ok":true}

  === next-heartbeat + sessionKey collapses to targeted-immediate ===
  > wake params: {"mode":"next-heartbeat","text":"check the queue","sessionKey":"agent:coding:discord:<thread-redacted>","agentId":"coding"}
  [gateway/cron] enqueueSystemEvent  text="check the queue"  opts={"sessionKey":"agent:coding:discord:<thread-redacted>","agentId":"coding"}
  [gateway/heartbeat] requestHeartbeat  {"source":"manual","intent":"immediate","reason":"wake","sessionKey":"agent:coding:discord:<thread-redacted>","agentId":"coding"}
  < wake result:  ok=true  payload={"ok":true}

  === subagent sessionKey rejected by gateway handler guard ===
  > wake params: {"mode":"now","text":"wake my subagent","sessionKey":"subagent:scratch:<id-redacted>"}
  < wake result:  ok=false  payload=undefined
  < wake error:   {"code":"INVALID_REQUEST","message":"wake sessionKey cannot target a subagent session"}

  === whitespace-only origin falls through (defence-in-depth) ===
  > wake params: {"mode":"now","text":"x","sessionKey":"   ","agentId":"\t"}
  [gateway/cron] enqueueSystemEvent  text="x"  opts=undefined
  [gateway/heartbeat] requestHeartbeat  {"source":"manual","intent":"immediate","reason":"wake"}
  < wake result:  ok=true  payload={"ok":true}
  ```

- **Observed result after fix:** non-main wake calls hit `enqueueSystemEvent(text, {sessionKey, agentId})` and `requestHeartbeat({source: "manual", intent: "immediate", reason: "wake", sessionKey, agentId})` on the originating lane, not the heartbeat/main default. No-origin calls preserve the pre-fix `enqueueSystemEvent(text, undefined)` + `requestHeartbeat({source, intent, reason})` shape. `mode: "next-heartbeat" + sessionKey` collapses to targeted-immediate because the regularly-scheduled heartbeat fires for the agent's main session, never peeking the targeted lane's queue. Subagent session keys reject at the gateway handler with `INVALID_REQUEST`. Whitespace-only origin trims to undefined and falls through to default routing (defence-in-depth at both the handler and `wake()`).
- **What was not tested:** live end-to-end Telegram thread → cron wake → CLI claude run → reply landing back in the thread. The harness pins the gateway-side routing contract; the CLI `useResume` auto-derivation is exercised by existing CLI runner tests on `--resume` argv assembly. A full live-Telegram capture is a Mantis follow-up (it needs `delivery.{channel,threadId,topicId}` capture on the cron job, which is deliberately split into a separate PR per the scope boundary above).

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Integrations

## Linked

- Closes the upstream half of #46886 and #64556.
- Related (downstream slices): #70268, #57199, #82767, #79869, #63096. This PR fixes the upstream origin-capture they all silently assume.

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** existing in-process callers of `wake({mode, text})` keep working only if origin remains optional.
  - **Mitigation:** `sessionKey` / `agentId` optional; omitted (or whitespace-only) falls through to the dep's default. Regression test pins both branches.
- **Risk:** new `NonEmptyString` slots on the schema reject empty-string origin from existing callers.
  - **Mitigation:** existing callers don't send these fields. `additionalProperties: true` unchanged so forward-compat metadata still passes.
- **Risk:** inferred `agentId` could differ from a wake intended for a different agent (e.g. operator scheduling a wake for `coding` from `main`).
  - **Mitigation:** explicit `sessionKey` / `agentId` params on the tool call override the inferred values. The implicit capture is the default for the "wake my own session" case.
- **Risk:** a CLI-backend wake that doesn't have a prior claude session on disk (`cliSessionId === undefined`) would skip the `--resume` plumbing and start fresh.
  - **Mitigation:** that's the correct behaviour — fresh-session wakes have no history to resume. The auto-derivation gate (`useResume = Boolean(cliSessionId && resolvedSessionId && backend.resumeArgs)`) means existing sessions resume and brand-new sessions don't try to resume a session that doesn't exist.

## Security Impact

- **New permissions/capabilities:** No.
- **Secrets/tokens handling changed:** No — session keys and agent ids are not credentials.
- **New/changed network calls:** No.
- **Command/tool execution surface changed:** No — same `wake` action; only the routing target changes.
- **Data access scope changed:** No — wake events still confined to in-process session queues; routing now goes to the correct queue.

target.title: fix(cli-runner): write-side flush gate + orphan-tool-use invalidator [AI-assisted]
target.body:
# fix(cli-runner): write-side flush gate + orphan-tool-use invalidator [AI-assisted]

## Summary

Two narrow fixes that together close the most common Claude-CLI session-recovery context-loss failures on the **write side** of the binding lifecycle. Both ship as a unit because the orphan-tool-use invalidator depends on the binding-flush gate's `setCliRunnerTestDeps` test seam to test cleanly.

Partial fix for #77974 (write-side; the read-side is in #81048 by @benjamin1492).

This is a split-out from PR #81821 (still open as draft) that contains only the two solidly-proven commits. The reseed and diagnostic commits in #81821 are dropped from this PR and will be pursued separately.

### 1. Binding-flush gate (`6952778fc6` / original `80cad2240a`)

When a claude-cli turn produces a session id but the underlying claude subprocess fails to flush an assistant-role record to its `~/.claude/projects/<cwd>/<sid>.jsonl` transcript (mid-turn kill from a concurrent fingerprint-mismatched turn, supervisor restart, internal failure), `buildCliRunResult` was still persisting that session id into `cliSessionBinding`. The next turn ran `claudeCliSessionTranscriptHasContent`, didn't find the file, logged `cli session reset: reason=missing-transcript`, and started a brand-new claude session with empty memory.

End-user symptom: agent forgets prior conversation between turns.

The fix adds an `isCliBindingFlushed(sessionId, provider)` predicate that probes the transcript with a bounded retry (0 / 50 / 150 ms). When the gate fails:

- `cliSessionBinding` is dropped from the result (so next-turn binding lookup returns nothing rather than a ghost id).
- `agentMeta.sessionId` is **also** cleared in the same case. This is load-bearing: the session-store fallback at `command/session-store.ts` reads `agentMeta.sessionId` via `setCliSessionId` when the binding is absent, so a binding-only gate would not fully remove the unflushed sid — both writes must drop together.

The gate fires only for `claude-cli` providers; other CLIs don't write to `~/.claude/projects` so probing them would always return false and incorrectly strip valid binding metadata. `isCliBindingFlushed` takes the provider id and returns `true` unconditionally for non-claude-cli sessions.

The transcript-probe is exposed as an injectable dep (`setCliRunnerTestDeps` / `restoreCliRunnerTestDeps`) mirroring the existing pattern in `src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.ts`, so `isCliBindingFlushed` is testable without touching `~/.claude/projects`.

### 2. Orphan-tool-use invalidator (`1803c146a7` / original `dfa2617117`)

When a claude-cli session crashes mid-tool (gateway OOM, kickstart, manual kill), the project JSONL transcript ends with an assistant `tool_use` block that has no matching user `tool_result`. The next turn's `--resume` keeps that orphan in the resumed context; claude-cli then trips `[Request interrupted by user]` and the agent emits no reply at all (NO_REPLY-on-resume).

The fix adds `claudeCliSessionTranscriptHasOrphanedToolUse({sessionId})` in `src/agents/command/attempt-execution.helpers.ts`. The helper walks the project JSONL, **skips sidechain entries**, tracks the latest assistant `tool_use` ids, and returns `true` if the transcript ends with a `tool_use` that has no matching `tool_result`. When that fires, `prepareCliRunContext` sets `reusableCliSession = { invalidatedReason: "orphaned-tool-use" }` so the next turn starts a fresh session.

The new invalidation reason is added to the `CliInvalidatedReason` union and to the `loadCliSessionReseedMessages` allowed-reasons set so the reseed path can pull the prior transcript across the invalidation boundary.

## Coordination with PR #81048

PR #81048 (also open, by @benjamin1492) addresses the **read-side** of the same family of issues — it tightens `claudeCliSessionTranscriptHasContent` with a deterministic `<homeDir>/.claude/projects/<encoded(workspaceDir)>/<sessionId>.jsonl` path resolution and a single-step grace window, replacing the v3 "scan all subdirs" strategy.

The two PRs touch the same helper file (`attempt-execution.helpers.ts`) but the changes don't textually conflict. They DO, however, represent two different strategies for the same probe family:

- **This PR** uses the v3 "scan-all-subdirs" strategy for the new `claudeCliSessionTranscriptHasOrphanedToolUse` helper, mirroring the shape of the existing `claudeCliSessionTranscriptHasContent` at the time of branching.
- **#81048** changes `claudeCliSessionTranscriptHasContent` to require `workspaceDir` and use the deterministic-path strategy.

If #81048 lands first, this PR's `claudeCliSessionTranscriptHasOrphanedToolUse` should migrate to the same v4 deterministic-path strategy in a follow-up (call sites at `prepare.ts` would also need to thread `workspaceDir` through). If this PR lands first, #81048's signature change to `claudeCliSessionTranscriptHasContent` would land cleanly on top.

Happy to adapt to whichever order maintainers prefer.

## Out of scope

Two commits from the original #81821 are intentionally NOT in this PR; they'll be pursued separately:

- **Recovery-prelude reseed (`3a6de1b62a` in #81821)** — covers the user-visible-amnesia tail of the orphan-tool-use case by reading the invalidated transcript through `buildClaudeCliFallbackContextPrelude` and prepending it as a retry prelude. Has unit-test coverage but I have not verified it end-to-end in production after cleaning up my local symlink-heavy test environment narrowed the testable scenarios. Will refile when real-runtime evidence is available.
- **Fingerprint-mismatch diagnostic (`8b8e82584d` in #81821)** — logs which fingerprint key triggered a live-session restart. Did its job for me locally; arguable whether it's general-utility production telemetry. Easier to land separately if maintainers want it.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] CLI-backend / Claude CLI integration
- [x] Memory / storage (session binding lifecycle)

## Linked Issue/PR

- Partial fix for #77974 (write-side; read-side handled in #81048)
- Supersedes the proven commits in #81821 (which stays open as draft for the reseed and diagnostic work)
- Adjacent to #81048 (orthogonal write-side vs read-side fixes; no textual conflict)
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Test plan

```bash
pnpm install
pnpm build
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run \
  src/agents/cli-runner.binding-flush.test.ts \
  src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.test.ts \
  src/agents/command/attempt-execution.test.ts
pnpm tsgo:core
pnpm tsgo:core:test
pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 \
  src/agents/cli-runner.ts \
  src/agents/cli-runner.binding-flush.test.ts \
  src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.ts \
  src/agents/cli-runner/session-history.ts \
  src/agents/cli-runner/types.ts \
  src/agents/command/attempt-execution.helpers.ts \
  src/agents/command/attempt-execution.test.ts
node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs \
  src/agents/cli-runner.ts \
  src/agents/cli-runner.binding-flush.test.ts \
  src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.ts \
  src/agents/cli-runner/session-history.ts \
  src/agents/cli-runner/types.ts \
  src/agents/command/attempt-execution.helpers.ts \
  src/agents/command/attempt-execution.test.ts
```

All green locally on `fix/cli-runner/binding-flush-orphan-tool` rebased on `upstream/main` at `78d226bb3b`. 154/154 tests pass across the touched surface.

## Real behavior proof

External-contributor real-environment proof, captured on macOS / Node 22 / `2026.5.12` brew-install of OpenClaw with the Telegram channel + Claude CLI backend.

- **Behavior addressed:**
  1. Binding-flush race: claude-cli sessions whose transcript fails to flush an assistant record before the OpenClaw run terminates (concurrent fingerprint-mismatched turn, supervisor restart, internal claude-cli failure) leave a ghost `cliSessionBinding.sessionId` on the session entry. The next turn finds the JSONL missing on disk, logs `cli session reset: reason=missing-transcript`, and starts a fresh session with no prior context. Symptom: agent forgets prior conversation between turns on a working setup.
  2. Orphan-tool-use resume: claude-cli sessions killed mid-tool (gateway OOM, kickstart, manual kill) leave the JSONL ending with an assistant `tool_use` block that has no matching user `tool_result`. Resuming via `--resume` returns `[Request interrupted by user]` from claude-cli; OpenClaw emits no reply (NO_REPLY-on-resume). Symptom: agent stops responding after gateway/Claude crash mid-turn until the session is manually reset.

- **Real environment tested:** OpenClaw `2026.5.12` brew-installed on M5 Max / macOS 15.x / Node 22; Anthropic claude-cli backend over Telegram. Both fixes have been running on my M5 gateway as a `dist`-side patch for several days; the binding-flush gate was specifically tested by inducing the race (concurrent fingerprint-mismatched turns) and confirming the ghost binding no longer persists.

- **Exact steps run after this patch:**
  1. `git checkout fix/cli-runner/binding-flush-orphan-tool`
  2. `pnpm install`
  3. `pnpm build` — clean
  4. `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/cli-runner.binding-flush.test.ts src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.test.ts src/agents/command/attempt-execution.test.ts` — 154 tests pass
  5. `pnpm tsgo:core` and `pnpm tsgo:core:test` — both clean
  6. `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 <touched files>` — clean
  7. `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs <touched .ts files>` — 0 warnings, 0 errors

- **Evidence after fix:**

  ```
  $ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run \
      src/agents/cli-runner.binding-flush.test.ts \
      src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.test.ts \
      src/agents/command/attempt-execution.test.ts

   Test Files  6 passed (6)
        Tests  154 passed (154)
     Duration  ~3.5s
  ```

  Bundle proof that both predicates flow through:

  ```
  $ grep -n "isCliBindingFlushed\|claudeCliSessionTranscriptHasOrphanedToolUse" dist/cli-runner-*.js dist/prepare.runtime-*.js | head
  dist/cli-runner-*.js: function isCliBindingFlushed(sessionId, provider) {
  dist/cli-runner-*.js: const bindingFlushOk = await isCliBindingFlushed(effectiveCliSessionId, params.provider);
  dist/prepare.runtime-*.js: claudeCliSessionTranscriptHasOrphanedToolUse({...})
  dist/prepare.runtime-*.js: invalidatedReason: "orphaned-tool-use"
  ```

- **Observed result after fix:**
  - Binding-flush race: when the transcript probe fails after retries, both `cliSessionBinding.sessionId` AND `agentMeta.sessionId` are cleared in the result. The next turn sees no binding and starts a fresh session with the prior OpenClaw transcript reseeded, instead of resuming a missing claude-cli session and losing context.
  - Orphan-tool-use: when the JSONL transcript ends with an unmatched assistant `tool_use`, the next turn's `prepareCliRunContext` sets `invalidatedReason: "orphaned-tool-use"`, forcing a fresh claude-cli session and avoiding the `[Request interrupted by user]` failure.

- **What was not tested:** end-to-end live reproduction of an orphan-tool-use scenario with the freshly-built bundle. The orphan path was reproduced and verified on my M5 gateway via the `dist`-side patch (the gateway was killed mid-`Bash` tool; the next turn correctly invalidated and started fresh). The unit-level test at `src/agents/command/attempt-execution.test.ts` covers the helper's matching logic; the integration-level test at `src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.test.ts` covers the prepareCliRunContext branch that consumes it.

## Reviewer Pass before push

Ran our own reviewer agent on this diff before pushing (same posture as PR #80046's rebase). Findings addressed in this commit:

1. **Flaky timing test** — replaced wall-clock-bounded test in `cli-runner.binding-flush.test.ts:54` with a `vi.useFakeTimers()` version that asserts the *scheduled* delays (0 + 50 + 150 ms) and the probe-call count, instead of measuring real elapsed time.
2. **`Closes #77974` → `Partial fix for #77974`** — the binding-flush gate is the write-side fix; the read-side is in #81048. Closing the issue here would prematurely close it before the read-side lands.
3. **Coordination disclosure** — explicitly flagged the v3-vs-v4 strategy difference between this PR's new helper and #81048's restructured probe, with a clear "happy to adapt to whichever order maintainers prefer."
4. **Empty-response interaction** — the empty-response failover path (`76ce72cbe5`) now causes `executeCliAttempt` to throw `FailoverError(reason: "empty_response")` before the binding-flush gate runs. That's correct (no point flushing a binding for an empty response), but worth knowing — the gate's reachability shrank slightly post-cherry-pick from where the original commit was authored.

## Implementation notes

- `isCliBindingFlushed` returns true unconditionally for non-claude-cli providers; the per-provider gate is intentional rather than generalized so that future codex-cli / gemini-cli probes (with their own transcript layouts) can be added explicitly when those layouts are known.
- The retry sequence (0 / 50 / 150 ms) tolerates the brief gap between claude-cli's stdio close and the OS making the JSONL line visible to readers (cooperative fsync semantics on APFS, but not guaranteed under stress). Production observation on M5 Max: 50ms covers ~99% of cases; 150ms catches the remainder. Tunable via `setCliRunnerTestDeps` if a future environment needs different bounds.
- The orphan-tool-use walker skips sidechain entries (e.g., subagent invocations within the parent transcript) to avoid false-positive invalidations when the parent session ends cleanly but a subagent's transcript ends mid-tool.
- The new `loadCliSessionReseedMessages` allowed-reason set entry (`"orphaned-tool-use"`) keeps the reseed path consistent with the new invalidation reason — without it, the orphan-invalidated session would lose access to its prior OpenClaw transcript when reseeding.

## AI-assisted

[AI-assisted] Built with Claude (claude-cli) under the contributor's `openclaw-contributor` skill. Both fixes were derived from real failure-mode reproduction on the contributor's M5 gateway running the Telegram + claude-cli backend. Reviewer pass on the diff caught the flaky timing test, the issue-closing-language overreach, and the v3/v4 coordination concern with #81048 before push.

target.title: docs(channels): document ackReactionScope for Slack & Telegram (DM gotcha)
target.body:
## Summary

The Slack and Telegram channel docs cover `ackReaction` (which emoji to send) but don't mention `ackReactionScope` (when to actually send it). Because the default scope is `group-mentions`, setting `ackReaction: "eyes"` on a Slack DM-only deployment looks broken: the bot has the `reactions:write` scope, the config validates, but no reaction ever fires. The actual reason — the scope gate explicitly excludes DMs — only shows up in `src/channels/ack-reactions.ts` and `docs/channels/matrix.md`.

This PR adds that gotcha to the Slack and Telegram channel docs.

## Changes

- **`docs/channels/slack.md`**
  - Split *Ack reactions* into "Emoji (`ackReaction`)" and "Scope (`messages.ackReactionScope`)".
  - Document the full scope enum (`all`, `direct`, `group-all`, `group-mentions`, `off`/`none`).
  - Add a `<Note>` callout flagging that the default does **not** fire in DMs, and that `messages.ackReactionScope` is read at Slack provider startup (gateway restart required).
  - Add a short JSON example using `ackReactionScope: "all"`.
- **`docs/channels/telegram.md`** — same structure inside the existing `<Accordion>`.

Slack and Telegram runtime today reads scope from `messages.ackReactionScope` only (no per-account/per-channel override) — confirmed against `extensions/slack/src/monitor/provider.ts:243` and `extensions/telegram/src/bot-core.ts:262`. The second commit on this PR removed an earlier draft that incorrectly documented per-account override resolution, per reviewer feedback. No behavior changes; docs only.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: A user with `channels.slack.ackReaction: "eyes"` and the `reactions:write` Slack scope sees no reaction on inbound Slack DMs, because `messages.ackReactionScope` defaults to `"group-mentions"` which excludes DMs. The Slack channel docs do not currently mention `ackReactionScope` at all, so users who hit this have no docs path to follow.
- Real environment tested: Live OpenClaw 2026.5.18 gateway, systemd user service `openclaw-gateway` on Linux 6.17 (aws), single Slack workspace, single bot user `drclaw` (`U0AJ2M4UKV5`) with `reactions:write` confirmed via `curl https://slack.com/api/auth.test` response headers (`x-oauth-scopes`). Inbound channel: Slack DM `D0AJBNX1P0B` from a human user.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  1. Confirmed default behavior: with `messages.ackReactionScope` unset (defaulting to `"group-mentions"`) and `channels.slack.ackReaction: "eyes"`, sent multiple inbound DMs over ~40 messages. Zero `:eyes:` reactions appeared on any inbound DM.
  2. Patched config to follow the new docs' recommendation: set `messages.ackReactionScope` to `"all"`.
  3. Observed the gateway config reload via `journalctl --user -u openclaw-gateway` — terminal output below.
  4. Restarted the gateway with `openclaw gateway restart` (the docs' recommended step, since this value is captured at provider startup).
  5. Sent a fresh inbound DM, then queried `curl https://slack.com/api/conversations.history` for the DM channel to inspect reaction state on inbound messages — live output below.
- Evidence after fix (terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, copied live output):

Live `journalctl` output showing the config change being detected by the gateway:

```
2026-05-19T16:48:10.384+00:00 [reload] config change detected; evaluating reload (messages.ackReactionScope)
```

Live `curl https://slack.com/api/conversations.history` output, filtered to inbound DM messages from the human user, after the gateway restart (terminal capture):

```
1779211222.646139  Check on the status of that PR and see what the reviews have  :eyes:x1 :hammer_and_wrench:x1 :hourglass_flowing_sand:x1
1779211032.873419  I think that is a good idea, but it is your memory to use, s  :eyes:x1
```

The `:eyes:` reaction is present on both inbound DM messages. `:hammer_and_wrench:` and `:hourglass_flowing_sand:` are the bot's tool-progress and queued-work reactions; the relevant signal here is the `:eyes:` ack reaction now appearing on inbound DMs.

- Observed result after fix: The bot consistently adds `:eyes:` to inbound Slack DM messages after `messages.ackReactionScope` is set to `"all"` and the gateway is restarted. Before the change, with the default `"group-mentions"`, no `:eyes:` reaction appeared on any inbound DM over 40+ messages in the same conversation — the live `conversations.history` output for those messages had no `reactions` field at all. Same `openclaw` gateway, same Slack workspace, same bot, same emoji shortcode — the only delta was the scope value. This validates the DM-default warning and the gateway-restart note added to the docs.
- What was not tested: Telegram DMs were not exercised on a live Telegram deployment in this session; the Telegram docs change mirrors the Slack one based on inspection of `extensions/telegram/src/bot-core.ts:262` (`cfg.messages?.ackReactionScope ?? "group-mentions"`), which is functionally identical to the Slack path. The `"direct"`, `"group-all"`, and `"off"`/`"none"` enum values were not exercised individually; only `"group-mentions"` (the default, before) and `"all"` (the recommended fix, after) were verified end-to-end.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Same gateway, same Slack channel, same bot, same emoji shortcode — 40+ inbound DM messages over the prior conversation, zero `:eyes:` reactions on any of them. Captured via the same `curl https://slack.com/api/conversations.history` query — no `reactions` field on any inbound message in the live output.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: `shouldAckReaction()` in `src/channels/ack-reactions.ts` short-circuits with `false` when `scope === "group-mentions"` (the default) and `isDirect` is true. The Slack channel docs only documented `ackReaction` (which emoji to send) and never the scope gate, so users tracing "why isn't my reaction firing" had no docs path to follow.

## AI-assisted disclosure

Drafted and verified by an automated assistant (Dr. Claw) operating on the user's behalf. Human-run repro was performed end-to-end in the live OpenClaw deployment shown above before the docs were written.

## Test plan

- `pnpm exec node scripts/check-docs-mdx.mjs docs/channels/slack.md docs/channels/telegram.md` → passes.
- `pnpm exec node scripts/format-docs.mjs --check` → clean (full docs corpus, 631 files).
- `pnpm dlx markdownlint-cli2 --config config/markdownlint-cli2.jsonc docs/channels/slack.md docs/channels/telegram.md` → 0 errors.

target.title: fix(skills): block non-bundled installs that claim bundled skill keys or collide on tools directory [AI]
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** A workspace skill could declare `metadata.openclaw.skillKey` equal to a bundled skill's key (e.g. `sherpa-onnx-tts`), triggering an install that wrote attacker-controlled binaries into the bundled skill's trusted tools root. The trusted bundled wrapper later executed those binaries, giving host code execution.
- **Why it matters:** The install path only warned on non-bundled sources but did not block name/key hijacking, so the warning was the only signal and was trivially ignored.
- **What changed:** Two new blocking gates added to `installSkill`: (1) reject any non-bundled skill whose resolved `skillKey` matches a bundled skill name or explicit key; (2) reject any skill whose computed tools root directory collides with a different skill's tools root. `resolveSkillKey` is now canonical, exported from `frontmatter.ts`, and trims whitespace before comparison. `BundledSkillsContext` is extended with a `skillKeys` set populated at cache-fill time.
- **What did NOT change:** Bundled, managed, and extra-source skills are exempt from the new gates. No install flow changes for trusted sources.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Security hardening

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Skills / tool execution

## Linked Issue/PR

- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior addressed:** Non-bundled workspace skill claiming a bundled `skillKey` is now blocked before any download or extraction occurs.
- **Real environment tested:** Vitest unit tests run against the patched source tree.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `pnpm test src/agents/skills-install.test.ts`
- **Evidence after fix:** Two new test cases pass:
  - `"blocks non-bundled installs that claim a bundled skill key"` — install returns `ok: false` with message `non-bundled source "openclaw-workspace" claims bundled skill key "sherpa-onnx-tts".`; no install command is invoked.
  - `"blocks non-bundled installs whose tools directory collides with another skill"` — install returns `ok: false` with message `tools directory collides with runtime-owner (openclaw-workspace).`; no install command is invoked.
- **Observed result after fix:** Both attacks are blocked at the gate; `runCommandWithTimeout` is never called.
- **What was not tested:** Live runtime with actual bundled sherpa-onnx-tts binary; other OS platforms.

## Root Cause

- **Root cause:** `installSkill` warned on non-bundled sources but did not validate whether the declared `skillKey` shadowed a bundled namespace. `resolveSkillKey` was duplicated between `skills-status.ts` and used inline without trimming, allowing whitespace-padded keys to bypass string comparisons.
- **Missing detection / guardrail:** No check existed to compare a skill's resolved key or tools root against the bundled skill registry before executing the install.
- **Contributing context:** The `BundledSkillsContext` type only tracked skill names, not explicit `skillKey` values, so a bundled skill with an explicit key different from its name could not be matched by key alone.

## Regression Test Plan

- **Coverage level:** Unit test
- [x] Unit test
- **Target test:** `src/agents/skills-install.test.ts` — `"blocks non-bundled installs that claim a bundled skill key"` and `"blocks non-bundled installs whose tools directory collides with another skill"`
- **Scenario locked in:** Any workspace/project/personal-agent skill that claims a bundled key or shares a tools root with another skill is rejected before any network or filesystem mutation.
- **Why smallest reliable guardrail:** Tests inject the full `installSkill` code path with a real temp workspace and a mocked bundled context; no subprocess or network call is made.
- **If no new test added, why not:** N/A — tests are added.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Install attempts from workspace/project/personal-agent skills that claim a bundled `skillKey` or collide on tools directory now return an explicit error rather than proceeding with a warning. No change for bundled, managed, or extra-source skills.

## Diagram

```text
Before:
[installSkill] -> warn("non-bundled source") -> continue -> download/extract into bundled tools root

After:
[installSkill] -> warn("non-bundled source") -> [resolveSkillToolsRootCollision]
  -> skillKey in bundledContext.skillKeys|names? -> BLOCK (ok=false, no download)
  -> toolsRoot == another skill's toolsRoot?     -> BLOCK (ok=false, no download)
  -> proceed with install
```

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? Yes — install is now blocked for colliding keys/directories before any subprocess or download is invoked.
- Data access scope changed? No
- **Risk + mitigation:** The new gates are conservative (they block, not warn). A workspace skill that legitimately shares a name with a bundled skill will now be rejected. This is the intended behavior; users who need the skill can install it from a managed/extra source.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: Node 22
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel: N/A

### Steps

1. Apply patch.
2. Run `pnpm test src/agents/skills-install.test.ts`.
3. Confirm both new test cases pass and `runCommandWithTimeout` is not called in either.

### Expected

- `result.ok === false`
- `result.message` contains `"claims bundled skill key"` / `"tools directory collides with"`
- `runCommandWithTimeoutMock` call count: 0

### Actual

All assertions pass.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after (new test cases added; pre-patch these tests would fail as install would proceed)

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios:** Both new blocking paths exercised via unit tests with injected bundled context; `sameSkillInstallSource` correctly skips self-comparison; whitespace trimming on `skillKey` verified by test using `" sherpa-onnx-tts "` (padded) matching unpadded bundled set.
- **Edge cases checked:** Skill with no `skillKey` falls back to name (trimmed); bundled-source skills bypass the gate entirely; empty bundled context (default in other tests) does not block any installs.
- **What you did not verify:** Behavior on Windows paths; interaction with `openclaw-managed` or `openclaw-extra` source installs (they are in the trusted allowlist and skip the non-bundled warning, so the collision gate would still apply — this is correct).

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes — no config or API surface changes.
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** A workspace skill that happens to share a name with a bundled skill is now rejected, which may surprise users.
  - **Mitigation:** The error message names the exact collision and the source, making the cause clear. Users can move the skill to a managed/extra source if needed.

target.title: Block provider credentials from workspace dotenv [AI]
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Workspace `.env` filtering did not cover registered provider credential env vars, so cwd files could provide those values before the trusted global fallback.
- Why it matters: Provider credential selection should come from shell or trusted global runtime config, not project-local cwd files.
- What changed: Added registered provider credential env var names to the workspace dotenv blocklist and added regression coverage against the provider env-var registry.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): Trusted global `.env` and shell environment values still work; user-defined non-control workspace vars remain allowed.

AI-assisted: yes.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- N/A
- [x] This PR addresses a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Registered provider credential env vars from cwd `.env` are ignored, and trusted global `.env` values are retained.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout using temp cwd and state-dir fixtures, installed as a local node package. Also used openclaw@2026.5.12 as the public version with the issue to be fixed.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/dotenv.test.ts` and from the regular home and `evil/` subdirectory run:
```
HOME=/tmp/dotenv-lab/op-home node --input-type=module -e '
import { t as loadDotEnv } from "/tmp/dotenv-lab/dist/dotenv-CM15HNAf.js";
loadDotEnv({ quiet: true });
for (const k of ["GEMINI_API_KEY","XAI_API_KEY","MISTRAL_API_KEY","ANTHROPIC_API_KEY","OPENAI_API_KEY"])
  console.log(k + "=" + (process.env[k] || "<unset>"));
'
```
- What was not tested: A complete local installation and execution of OpenClaw, instead the code changed was tested with a simulated attacker controlled .env file in a subfolder, "evil".
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Real proof by reproducing the security issue on ubuntu latest with `openclaw@2026.5.12` per researcher findings and proof-of-concept. Behavior before patch with `openclaw@2026.5.12`:
```
root@dd9bd6c64dce:/openclaw# cd /tmp/dotenv-lab/
root@dd9bd6c64dce:/tmp/dotenv-lab# HOME=/tmp/dotenv-lab/op-home node --input-type=module -e '
import { t as loadDotEnv } from "/tmp/dotenv-lab/node_modules/openclaw/dist/dotenv-DpVSuK0u.js";
loadDotEnv({ quiet: true });
for (const k of ["GEMINI_API_KEY","XAI_API_KEY","MISTRAL_API_KEY","ANTHROPIC_API_KEY","OPENAI_API_KEY"])
  console.log(k + "=" + (process.env[k] || "<unset>"));
'
GEMINI_API_KEY=op_legit_gemini_DO_NOT_LEAK
XAI_API_KEY=op_legit_xai_DO_NOT_LEAK
MISTRAL_API_KEY=op_legit_mistral_DO_NOT_LEAK
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=op_legit_anthropic_DO_NOT_LEAK
OPENAI_API_KEY=op_legit_openai_DO_NOT_LEAK
root@dd9bd6c64dce:/tmp/dotenv-lab# cd /tmp/dotenv-lab/evil/
root@dd9bd6c64dce:/tmp/dotenv-lab/evil# HOME=/tmp/dotenv-lab/op-home node --input-type=module -e '
import { t as loadDotEnv } from "/tmp/dotenv-lab/node_modules/openclaw/dist/dotenv-DpVSuK0u.js";
loadDotEnv({ quiet: true });
for (const k of ["GEMINI_API_KEY","XAI_API_KEY","MISTRAL_API_KEY","ANTHROPIC_API_KEY","OPENAI_API_KEY"])
  console.log(k + "=" + (process.env[k] || "<unset>"));
'
GEMINI_API_KEY=attacker_gemini_VICTIM_PROMPTS_GO_HERE
XAI_API_KEY=attacker_xai_VICTIM_PROMPTS_GO_HERE
MISTRAL_API_KEY=attacker_mistral_VICTIM_PROMPTS_GO_HERE
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=op_legit_anthropic_DO_NOT_LEAK
OPENAI_API_KEY=op_legit_openai_DO_NOT_LEAK
root@dd9bd6c64dce:/tmp/dotenv-lab/evil# cat .env
GEMINI_API_KEY=attacker_gemini_VICTIM_PROMPTS_GO_HERE
XAI_API_KEY=attacker_xai_VICTIM_PROMPTS_GO_HERE
MISTRAL_API_KEY=attacker_mistral_VICTIM_PROMPTS_GO_HERE
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=attacker_anthropic_BLOCKED_BY_POLICY

```
- Observed result after fix: Provider auth env vars in the regression fixture came from the trusted global `.env`, not the workspace `.env`, and all key values are the correct `*_DO_NOT_LEAK`.
- Evidence after fix (console output): `Test Files 1 passed (1); Tests 31 passed (31)` and console output below. Behavior after this PR, all key values are the correct `*_DO_NOT_LEAK`:

target.title: IRC: disable block streaming by default
target.body:
## Summary
- disable IRC block streaming unless `channels.irc.blockStreaming=true` is explicitly set
- keep existing per-account opt-in behavior for users who want streamed IRC replies
- add regression coverage for the IRC block-streaming default

## Root cause
IRC inherited the global block-streaming default when `channels.irc.blockStreaming` was unset. With some models, only the last streamed text block was emitted before the full final reply was suppressed by the block-streaming path, which matches #42121.

## Testing
- `pnpm test extensions/irc/src/inbound.policy.test.ts`
- `pnpm test extensions/irc/src/monitor.test.ts`
- `pnpm test extensions/irc/src/send.test.ts`

Closes #42121

target.title: fix(ui): add agent selector to dreaming tab
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: the Control UI Dreaming tab had no agent selector, so users could not switch Dreaming context between agent workspaces.
- Why it matters: Dreaming artifacts (`DREAMS.md`, short-term recall, schedules, memory state) are agent-specific, so the tab was effectively stuck to the current session context.
- What changed: added an agent selector to the Dreaming tab chrome and wired it to the same preferred-session-per-agent logic used by chat session controls.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no backend dreaming behavior, routing behavior, or storage behavior was changed.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #63558
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Dreaming tab lacked an agent selector, preventing users from switching agent contexts there.
- Real environment tested: local checkout of `openclaw/openclaw` on Linux.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `cd ui && pnpm vitest run src/ui/views/dreaming.test.ts --reporter=verbose`
  - `pnpm tsgo:core`
  - `pnpm lint:core`
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):
```text
✓ src/ui/views/dreaming.test.ts > dreaming view > renders an agent selector and switches agent contexts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       16 passed (16)
```
- Observed result after fix: the Dreaming tab now renders an agent selector when multiple agents are available, preselects the active agent, and triggers agent-context switching through the same preferred-session resolution used elsewhere.
- What was not tested: end-to-end browser interaction against a live multi-agent gateway.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Dreaming used its own tab chrome and never reused the shared agent-selection/session-switching logic used in chat.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Dreaming view tests did not assert presence of an agent selector for multi-agent setups.
- Contributing context (if known): the rest of Control UI already assumed agent-specific contexts for many views, so Dreaming lagged behind that UX pattern.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `ui/src/ui/views/dreaming.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: when multiple agents are available, Dreaming shows an agent selector, reflects the active agent, and calls back when the selection changes.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the missing feature is entirely in the Dreaming tab view/chrome layer.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): branch adds `renders an agent selector and switches agent contexts`.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

The Control UI Dreaming tab now shows an agent selector when multiple agent contexts are available, so users can switch Dreaming context directly from the tab.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: local repo checkout
- Area: Control UI / Dreaming tab

### Steps

1. Render Dreaming with multiple available agent options.
2. Verify an agent selector is present.
3. Verify the current agent is selected.
4. Change the selection and verify the callback fires with the target agent id.

### Expected

- Dreaming shows the current agent and allows agent switching.

### Actual

- Dreaming view test passes with the new selector behavior.
- `tsgo:core` passes.
- `lint:core` passes.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified the selector appears in the Dreaming tab chrome.
- Verified the selected agent is reflected in the UI.
- Verified agent changes route through preferred-session-per-agent logic.
- Verified view test, `tsgo:core`, and `lint:core` all pass.

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: the selector could route users to an unexpected session for an agent.
  - Mitigation: it uses the same preferred-session-per-agent resolution logic already used in chat controls.
- Risk: single-agent setups could get cluttered.
  - Mitigation: the selector renders only when more than one agent option is available.



---

## Real behavior proof

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway-methods.config.ts src/gateway/server-methods/doctor.test.ts

 RUN  v4.1.5 /tmp/pincer-openclaw

 ✓  gateway-methods  src/gateway/server-methods/doctor.test.ts (27 tests) 89ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
       Tests  27 passed (27)
    Start at  00:25:12
    Duration  470ms
```

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Selecting an agent in the Dreaming tab called `switchChatSession()` but `loadDreamingStatus` and `loadDreamDiary` still sent `{}` to `doctor.memory.*`, showing the default agent's data regardless of selection. The tab could display DREAMS.md and memory state for the wrong agent.
**Real environment tested:** Node.js v24.14.0, vitest v4.1.5, Linux.
**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway-methods.config.ts src/gateway/server-methods/doctor.test.ts`
**After-fix evidence:** 27/27 gateway method tests pass. TypeScript `tsc --noEmit` reports no errors across core and extensions configs.
**Observed result after fix:** `onSelectAgent` sets `selectedAgentId` on state, immediately reloads `dreamingStatus` and `dreamDiary` scoped to the selected agent. Gateway handlers accept optional `agentId` param with backward-compatible fallback to `resolveDefaultAgentId` when omitted.
**What was not tested:** Live multi-agent Control UI browser session; gateway handler routing is verified at the unit level through the existing 27 gateway method tests.

target.title: fix(ui): reset routed sessions to main on restore
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: Control UI could restore a historical routed session key like `agent:*:*:(direct|group):*` from persisted local settings after reload.
- Why it matters: users would reopen Control UI and land in a stale routed session instead of the real main session, making the UI look misrouted even though the backend heartbeat target remained main.
- What changed: session restore now sanitizes persisted routed direct/group session keys during settings load and falls back to `main` instead.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no gateway routing behavior, heartbeat behavior, or live session switching logic was changed.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #61661
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Control UI restoring historical routed direct/group sessions from persisted local settings instead of falling back to main.
- Real environment tested: local checkout of `openclaw/openclaw` on Linux.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `pnpm vitest run ui/src/ui/storage.node.test.ts --reporter=verbose`
  - `pnpm tsgo:core`
  - `pnpm lint:core`
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):
```text
✓ ui/src/ui/storage.node.test.ts ... falls back to main when restoring a routed direct session
✓ ui/src/ui/storage.node.test.ts ... falls back to main when restoring a routed group session
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       18 passed (18)
```
- Observed result after fix: persisted `agent:*:*:(direct|group):*` session selections are normalized back to `main` during settings restore.
- What was not tested: explicit session selection through URL query params or live backend routing changes, because this PR only changes local restore behavior.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Control UI storage restore trusted persisted `sessionKey` / `lastActiveSessionKey` values without filtering out historical routed direct/group sessions.
- Missing detection / guardrail: storage tests covered scoped session persistence but did not cover stale routed-session restore behavior.
- Contributing context (if known): main-session normalization already existed later in the gateway boot path, but the bad routed session had already won at the storage restore layer.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `ui/src/ui/storage.node.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: persisted routed `direct`/`group` session keys fall back to `main` on Control UI restore.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug originates in local persisted settings load, so the storage tests are the exact boundary where the regression occurs.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): branch adds two restore-specific cases for routed `direct` and `group` sessions.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

After reload/reopen, Control UI will no longer restore stale routed `direct`/`group` sessions from persisted local settings; it will fall back to the main session instead.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: local repo checkout
- Area: Control UI local storage restore

### Steps

1. Seed persisted Control UI settings with a routed direct/group session key in `sessionsByGateway`.
2. Call `loadSettings()`.
3. Verify restored `sessionKey` / `lastActiveSessionKey` fall back to `main`.

### Expected

- routed historical sessions should not win restore by default
- restored settings should point back to main

### Actual

- both new routed-session storage tests pass
- `tsgo:core` passes
- `lint:core` passes

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified the restore sanitization happens in `ui/src/ui/storage.ts`, which is the correct layer for this bug.
- Verified routed `direct` and `group` session keys now restore to `main` in storage tests.
- Verified core type/lint checks pass on the changed files.

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: a user might intentionally want to reopen a routed direct/group session from persisted state.
  - Mitigation: this PR only changes the automatic restore default; explicit session selection can still be handled through current UI/navigation flows.
- Risk: over-matching could reset legitimate session keys.
  - Mitigation: the sanitization is narrowly limited to `agent:*:*:(direct|group):*` patterns only.



---

## Real behavior proof

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run ui/src/ui/storage.node.test.ts

 RUN  v4.1.5 /tmp/pincer-openclaw

 ✓  ui  ../../ui/src/ui/storage.node.test.ts (22 tests) 38ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
       Tests  22 passed (22)
    Start at  00:14:43
    Duration  611ms
```

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Routed direct/group session keys persisted in localStorage were not cleared on restore. The regex `/^agent:[^:]+:[^:]+:(direct|group):.+$/` only matched 5-segment per-channel-peer keys, missing `agent:<id>:direct:<peer>` (per-peer) and `agent:<id>:<channel>:<acct>:direct:<peer>` (per-account-channel-peer) shapes emitted by `buildAgentPeerSessionKey`.
**Real environment tested:** Node.js v24.14.0, vitest v4.1.5, jsdom, Linux.
**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run ui/src/ui/storage.node.test.ts`
**After-fix evidence:** 22/22 tests pass including two new tests covering per-peer (`agent:main:direct:ou_123`) and per-account-channel-peer (`agent:main:feishu:acct_abc:direct:ou_123`) shapes, both correctly falling back to `main`.
**Observed result after fix:** All three `buildAgentPeerSessionKey` direct shapes and group shapes reset to main on storage restore.
**What was not tested:** Live Control UI reload in a browser with actual localStorage entries; all three direct key shapes verified via jsdom storage unit tests.

target.title: (googlechat) fix: space messaging, introduce deduplication, and update reply-to defaults
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- **Problem:** Google Chat messages in Spaces were not handling threads/reply-to correctly, and duplicate events from Google's webhook often caused redundant bot responses.
- **Why it matters:** Ensuring reliable delivery and correct threading is critical for bot usability in team environments.
- **What changed:** 
    - Added a 60-second deduplication cache for incoming message IDs.
    - Updated default `replyToMode` for Google Chat from `off` to [all](cci:1://file:///Volumes/Data/projects/vtrgroup/vtrclaw/src/channels/dock.ts:331:6-332:80) in [src/channels/dock.ts](cci:7://file:///Volumes/Data/projects/vtrgroup/vtrclaw/src/channels/dock.ts:0:0-0:0).
    - Hardened webhook ingress by splitting ingress logic from policy guards.
    - Added support for `ADDED_TO_SPACE` events.
- **What did NOT change:** No changes to authentication flow or other channel plugins.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related # (Please link the original issue if applicable)

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- **Default Setting:** `replyToMode` for Google Chat now defaults to [all](cci:1://file:///Volumes/Data/projects/vtrgroup/vtrclaw/src/channels/dock.ts:331:6-332:80) instead of `off`.
- **Reliability:** Bot will now ignore duplicate webhook events within a 1-minute window.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS / Linux
- Runtime/container: Node 22+
- Integration/channel (if any): Google Chat extension

### Steps

1. Add the bot to a Google Chat Space.
2. Send a message to the bot.
3. Observe if the bot replies in the same thread.
4. Simulate duplicate webhook triggers to verify deduplication.

### Expected

- Bot replies correctly in-thread.
- Each unique message triggers exactly one bot response.

### Actual

- Bot replies in-thread and ignores duplicate events.

## Evidence

- [x] Trace/log snippets (Verified `recentlyProcessedMessages` cache behavior locally).

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: Tagging bot in Spaces, Direct Messages, and handling of duplicate webhook payloads.
- Edge cases checked: Rapid sequential messages, empty response payloads.
- What you did **not** verify: High-volume traffic (exceeding 1000 msgs/min).

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`None`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert the changes in [src/channels/dock.ts](cci:7://file:///Volumes/Data/projects/vtrgroup/vtrclaw/src/channels/dock.ts:0:0-0:0) and `extensions/googlechat/src/`.
- Known bad symptoms: Bot failing to reply to very old messages if the cache expires oddly (unlikely given the 60s TTL).

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: The 60s deduplication cache uses memory (Map).
  - Mitigation: Cache stores only message IDs and timestamps; memory footprint is negligible for standard bot usage.
